title: Dog Jaw Locked Open: Causes, Urgency, and Next Steps
meta_desc: If your dog's jaw is locked open, understand why it happens, the urgency of the situation, and how to help your vet with triage through photos.
content_html:
What Is an Open-Mouth Jaw Lock?

When a dog is unable to close its mouth, it is most frequently caused by a condition known as TMJ (temporomandibular joint) luxation or open-mouth jaw locking. This happens when the joint that connects the lower jaw to the skull slips out of place or gets caught on the cheekbones. This can occur due to trauma, chewing on large objects, or underlying joint laxity.

Urgency Level: Medium

A jaw locked open is considered a medium-priority emergency. While it is rarely immediately life-threatening, it is extremely painful, stressful, and prevents your dog from eating or drinking. You should contact your veterinarian or an emergency clinic immediately to have the jaw safely reset under sedation. If you notice any respiratory distress or if the mouth is blocked by a foreign object, the urgency increases.

The Importance of Photos for Triage

Providing your veterinarian with a clear photo or video of your dog’s face and the angle of the jaw can be incredibly helpful for remote triage. These visuals allow the medical team to see if there is visible swelling, facial asymmetry, or a potential foreign object. This helps them prepare the appropriate sedation and diagnostic tools, such as X-rays, before you even arrive at the clinic.

faqs.question: Should I try to force my dog's jaw shut?
faqs.answer: No, never attempt to force the jaw closed. This can cause severe pain, worsen the joint injury, or result in an accidental bite. Professional sedation is almost always required to relax the muscles and reset the joint.

faqs.question: Is a locked jaw painful for dogs?
faqs.answer: Yes, this condition is typically very painful. You may notice your dog pawing at their mouth, drooling excessively, or acting restless and anxious.

faqs.question: What are the most common causes of this condition?
faqs.answer: The most common causes include TMJ luxation (dislocation), a foreign object like a stick or bone wedged in the teeth, or severe dental disease affecting the joint.
